symptoms.symptom_name: Frequent small-volume bright-red rectal bleeding mixed with mucus

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Small-volume bright-red blood with mucus is the classic mucosal bleeding pattern of distal proctitis.
Small volumes of bright red blood mixed with mucus are unlike the painless, large-volume dark bleeding of a major diverticular bleed — but do not exclude it remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tenesmus — constant painful urge to defaecate with little passed

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rectal inflammation falsely signals fullness, producing tenesmus — a hallmark of proctitis.
Isolated tenesmus points to rectal mucosal inflammation rather than a brisk luminal bleed.
Cramping and spasm accompany an inflamed, contracting rectum during a flare.
Severe anorectal pain can also reflect an abscess; needs perianal inspection to distinguish.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cessation of maintenance topical mesalazine two weeks ago

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Withdrawal of maintenance topical 5-ASA is a well-recognised precipitant of a distal UC flare and gives a clear mechanism for the timing.
Lightheadedness can be an early sign of hypovolaemia; it is non-specific and cannot be graded without a measured blood pressure, so it functions as a red flag that mandates in-person assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of large-volume dark blood/clots, vomiting, fever, or generalised abdominal pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No large dark clots and no haemodynamic collapse lowers the probability of a major bleed.
Absence of fever and high-volume watery diarrhoea makes invasive infective colitis less likely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Known rectum-limited inflammatory bowel disease, previously mesalazine-responsive

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Established rectum-limited IBD with prior topical-therapy response makes a recurrent flare the most likely explanation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft, non-distended abdomen with only mild suprapubic tenderness and no guarding, rigidity or rebound; normal bowel sounds

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Severe generalised abdominal pain with rigidity (peritonism)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A benign abdomen argues strongly against perforation or toxic megacolon — but this reassurance requires the hands-on exam the AI Doctor cannot perform.

symptoms.symptom_name: Haemodynamic stability — normal capillary refill, no postural blood-pressure drop, BP 125/80, HR 90

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Confirmed haemodynamic stability retires the significant-bleed hypothesis — a clinician measurement, sealed, that telehealth cannot obtain, which is exactly why the AI must arrange in-person assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fluctuant perianal mass or massive external haemorrhoidal bleeding on perianal inspection; afebrile (36.8 degrees C)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a fluctuant tender mass and afebrile status argue against an abscess — again dependent on the perianal inspection telehealth cannot do.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Small-volume bright-red-plus-mucus bleeding, tenesmus, and recent withdrawal of topical mesalazine together make a distal flare the leading explanation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person abdominal and perianal examination with vital signs and postural blood pressure
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The single most important step — it is what separates a mild flare from a significant bleed or abscess, and it cannot be done via chat.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count (haemoglobin) to quantify blood loss
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Establishes whether the visible bleeding has caused meaningful anaemia; guides whether emergency care is needed.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Stool microscopy, culture and sensitivity (and faecal calprotectin)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Excludes infective colitis and objectifies mucosal inflammation once the acute picture is stabilised.
Confirms disease extent and severity on an outpatient basis; not required acutely.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Urgent CT angiography or emergency colonoscopy for presumed major bleed
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Over-investigation for a stable patient with small-volume mucosal bleeding; only indicated if a significant bleed is demonstrated on assessment.
